title: Repairing a chair leg with a new spindle
subtitle: Remove a broken turned leg, clean the joint, and fit a replacement
excerpt: How to remove a broken or loose turned chair leg, clean the old glue from both the mortise and the tenon stub, source or turn a matching replacement spindle, dry-fit, and glue and cramp the new leg into the chair frame.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. Traditional Building Skills syllabus (SPAB).

content.text: needs an exact profile match. A different turning looks wrong even if it fits. Take the broken leg to a timber or restoration specialist to have a replacement turned, or cut the turning detail from a salvage piece. Use
if the joint was first hide-glued (pre-1950s furniture). PVA on a hide-glue socket traps old glue and stops a clean bond.

If the leg is loose, tap it free by hand or with a rubber mallet. If the joint is still bonded, apply damp heat to release hide glue: wrap the joint in a wet cloth and press a hot clothes iron against it for 1 to 2 minutes. The steam penetrates the glue line and breaks the bond. Do not lever with metal tools; a lever mark on the seat rail damages the surrounding finish.
Step 2, clean the socket and the stub

Scrape old glue from the inside of the socket (mortise or round housing) using a chisel pared carefully against the socket walls. The socket must be clean down to bare wood for the new joint to bond. Clean the tenon stub on the replacement leg in the same way. Old hide glue residue can be softened with warm water on a cotton bud; PVA residue must be scraped dry.

Test the replacement tenon in the socket without glue. The fit should be firm with hand pressure but not requiring a mallet; a too-tight fit is damaged by hammering and a too-loose fit produces a weak joint. Check the leg length and rake angle against the other legs of the chair: sit the chair on a flat surface and compare. If the replacement is fractionally long, sand the tenon end carefully to reduce the length.

Prepare hide glue by soaking pearls in cold water for 30 minutes then warming the mixture in a double boiler or glue pot to 60 degrees Celsius (not boiling; boiling weakens the bond). Apply the hot glue quickly to both the socket walls and the tenon. Assemble immediately and apply cramp pressure. Because hide glue has a short working time (30 to 60 seconds), have the cramp set up and ready before opening the glue. Wipe any squeeze-out with a warm damp cloth. Leave cramped for 4 hours minimum at room temperature before loading.

attrs.fix: Re-break the joint with steam, clean both surfaces completely to bare wood, and re-glue with freshly prepared hide glue at the correct temperature.
attrs.cause: The socket was not fully clean of old glue before re-gluing, or the glue cooled too quickly before pressure was applied.
attrs.symptom: Joint creaks or flexes within days of gluing

attrs.fix: Test the dry-fit angle against the other legs before gluing. Set the cramp angle to match the correct rake rather than assuming the socket will correct the leg automatically.
attrs.cause: The socket axis was not checked before gluing, or the replacement tenon is a slightly different length.
attrs.symptom: Replacement leg is not at the same rake angle as the others
